summary: Macro base rates for May 2026–May 2027 show modestly favorable conditions for Archer: Fed rate cuts (50 bp) lower capital costs, inflation moderates (PCE -50 bp), but labor scarcity persists (wage growth 3.1–3.4%) and electricity costs rise (+8–14%), creating mixed COGS pressure. GDP growth remains modest (2.3%), making execution—not macro—the key driver of A2.3 forward outcomes.

extras.next_year_value: 3.0–3.25% (projected Dec 2027, per FOMC March 2026 dot plot)
extras.relevance_to_a23: Capital availability gate (A2.3 Driver 1 & 3). Lower rates reduce cost of Archer's debt financing and customer capex financing. 50 bp decline modestly improves capital markets access, offsetting some refinancing pressure.
extras.policy_implication: Two 25 bp cuts expected in 2026, two cuts in 2027 per dot plot. Fed cites inflation moderation and stable labor market as justification.
extras.forecast_source: FOMC Summary of Economic Projections, March 18, 2026

extras.relevance_to_a23: Production cost and demand gate (A2.3 Driver 1 & 3). Lower inflation eases materials cost pressures (composites, electronics) and improves eVTOL value proposition vs. traditional aviation. Net positive for revenue launch and battery cost targets.
extras.current_context: Inflation 3.4% YoY as of March 2026, partly driven by geopolitical energy price spikes (Iran Strait of Hormuz conflict). Expected to moderate as Fed cuts rates.
extras.forecast_source: FOMC Summary of Economic Projections, March 18, 2026

extras.metric: Employment Cost Index for wages and salaries (% YoY)
extras.next_year_value: 3.1% (ASSUMED, based on unemployment moderating to 4.2% by end-2027 per Fed SEP)
extras.relevance_to_a23: Production COGS gate (A2.3 Driver 1 & 3). Aerospace sector faces acute labor shortages (56% of mfg firms report hiring difficulty for skilled trades); wage growth remains elevated. At 3.1–3.4% YoY, labor cost inflation will compress unit economics for aircraft production unless pricing increases or manufacturing efficiency improves 150–170 bp annually.
extras.structural_constraint: Aerospace workforce 29% above age 55; retirements accelerate. Aerospace sector needs 3.8M jobs by 2033. Tight labor market (unemployment 4.2% end-2027 vs. 4.0% neutral) means shortages persist.
extras.forecast_source: BLS ECI current; forward assumption based on Fed unemployment projection (March 2026 SEP)

rows.label: US GDP Growth (Demand Foundation)
rows.value: 2.4% (2026 Fed forecast) → 2.3% (2027 Fed forecast); flat with slight deceleration

extras.relevance_to_a23: Revenue demand gate (A2.3 Driver 1). Modest 2.3% growth supports baseline corporate capex and airline spending but does not accelerate demand for premium transportation. Revenue launch depends on execution (United/USAF operational readiness) rather than macro acceleration.
extras.range_uncertainty: Goldman Sachs projects 2.5% (higher); Deloitte projects 0.4% contraction 2027 (lower tail risk). Consensus mid-point 2.3% is durable against near-term shocks.
extras.forecast_source: FOMC Summary of Economic Projections, March 18, 2026; cross-triangulated with Goldman Sachs, Deloitte

extras.next_year_value: 4.0% (projected by end-2026/early-2027, Long Forecast; further declines to 3.98% by Sept 2027)
extras.relevance_to_a23: Non-dilutive capital cost gate (A2.3 Driver 3). Lower 10-year yields reduce cost of Archer's long-term debt, making debt financing more attractive vs. equity raises. A 37 bp decline lowers 10-year debt service by ~$3.7M per $1B borrowed annually. Net positive for reducing dilution pressure.
extras.market_dynamics: Decline driven by expected Fed rate cuts (50 bp over 12 months) and moderating inflation expectations. Subject to repricing if macro surprises emerge.

extras.metric: ICE BofA US High Yield Index Option-Adjusted Spread (bp)
extras.next_year_value: 300–350 bp (late 2026/early 2027, consensus forecast; mid-point assumed 325 bp)
extras.relevance_to_a23: Capital availability refinancing risk (A2.3 Driver 3). Spread widening (+45 bp mid-point) increases cost of debt for non-dilutive capital raises. However, widening is offset by Fed rate cuts (50 bp), leaving net cost of capital roughly flat or slightly lower. Refinancing wall looming (>$700B due 2027–2029) creates pressure, but Archer's strong liquidity (~$2B) insulates it from near-term stress.
extras.market_condition: Spreads currently at 25-year tights, indicating strong risk appetite and low default expectations. Widening reflects macro uncertainty and supply pressures (M&A, AI infrastructure financing), not distress.
extras.forecast_source: LPL Research, PitchBook, JP Morgan consensus (2026–2027 outlook)

rows.label: Brent Crude Oil Price (eVTOL Value Proposition)

extras.current_year_value: $81/bbl avg Q1 2026, peaks $115/bbl Q2 2026 (Iran Strait of Hormuz conflict), declines to $88/bbl avg Q4 2026 (EIA April 2026 STEO)
extras.next_year_value: $76/bbl avg 2027, declining toward $64/bbl by end-2027 (EIA long-term outlook)
extras.relevance_to_a23: Revenue demand and unit economics gate (A2.3 Driver 1). Lower oil prices narrow the fuel cost advantage of electric aviation relative to traditional helicopter/jet fuel, but do not eliminate it. eVTOL electricity cost (~$0.05–0.08/mile) remains well below Jet-A equivalent (~$0.12–0.15/mile) even at $76/bbl oil. Declining prices moderately reduce customer willingness-to-pay for premium eVTOL service, but baseline value proposition holds.
extras.geopolitical_risk: Forecast depends on resolution of Iran Strait of Hormuz conflict and OPEC production decisions. Spike to $115/bbl in Q2 2026 is transitory; baseline assumes eventual moderation.

extras.metric: Regional Wholesale Electricity Price, Load-Weighted Average (USD/MWh)
extras.current_year_value: $51/MWh (2026 forecast, EIA; represents 8.5% increase from 2025 baseline $47/MWh)
extras.next_year_value: $55–58/MWh (ASSUMED 2027, based on continued 8–14% annual growth driven by data center AI demand, grid decarbonization, renewable capacity additions)
extras.relevance_to_a23: Battery manufacturing cost gate (A2.3 Driver 3). Rising electricity costs increase COGS for solid-state battery producers. [A2.3 identifies 400 Wh/kg delivery as HIGH sensitivity, non-linear gating function]. Higher electricity costs may delay cost targets or require higher battery prices, affecting eVTOL unit economics.
extras.demand_drivers: Data center electricity demand growing fastest (AI infrastructure), driving wholesale prices higher. Renewable energy additions (21% solar growth 2026–2027) require transmission infrastructure investment, raising marginal generation costs.
extras.forecast_source: EIA Short-Term Energy Outlook, April 2026; extrapolation based on historical growth trends

rows.label: Venture Capital Funding Market (Capital Availability for eVTOL)
rows.value: $375B (2026 US forecast) → $400B+ (ASSUMED 2027); modest growth but concentrated in AI

extras.metric: U.S. Venture Capital Funding (USD billions, annual)
extras.current_year_value: $375B (2026 forecast, Crunchbase; represents recovery from 2024–2025 downturn)
extras.relevance_to_a23: Capital availability gate (A2.3 Driver 3). VC market recovery supports growth-stage companies like Archer, but capital is concentrating in AI-focused firms. eVTOL benefits from White House pilot program support and Archer's already-strong $2B liquidity position, reducing near-term dilution risk. If VC capital dries up beyond 2027, future raises could be more dilutive.
extras.sector_dynamics: AI companies attracting 85% of global VC funding, 53% of deals. Non-AI sectors (including aerospace/eVTOL) face tighter capital, but strategic partnerships (Stellantis manufacturing, Boeing investment) provide alternative funding channels.
extras.forecast_source: Crunchbase Predictions 2026–2027; Wellington Management VC Outlook

rows.label: US Unemployment Rate (Labor Market Tightness & Wage Inflation)

extras.relevance_to_a23: Labor supply and wage inflation gate (A2.3 Driver 1 & 3). Unemployment remains above Fed's estimated neutral rate (4.0%), indicating tight labor market persists. Aerospace sector faces structural shortages (56% of mfg firms struggle to hire skilled workers; 29% of workforce above age 55). Modest unemployment decline (20 bp) unlikely to resolve aerospace-specific labor scarcity. Implication: wage growth stays elevated (3.1–3.4% YoY), compressing unit COGS for aircraft production unless manufacturing efficiency improves or pricing increases.
extras.structural_constraint: Aerospace needs 3.8M jobs by 2033; current shortages in machinists, CNC operators, welders show no sign of abating. Production ramp-up (33 aircraft 2026 → 465 by 2030 per Archer guidance) will compete fiercely for skilled labor.
extras.forecast_source: FOMC Summary of Economic Projections, March 18, 2026
